title: Sila raises $300 million to expand US silicon anode production - electrive.com
content:
US battery materials specialist Sila has secured $300 million in fresh private funding to expand production of its silicon-carbon (Si/C) anode technology. The company plans to accelerate the next phase of its Moses Lake plant, targeting applications including electric vehicles.
Image: Sila Nanotechnologies
The California-based battery developer and Mercedes partner says it will use the capital to scale production of its Titan Silicon anode material in the United States. The funding will also support the second expansion phase of Sila’s production site in Moses Lake. The company completed construction of the facility and started operations
in autumn 2025.
Located on a 160-acre site, the plant currently has an initial production capacity equivalent to 2 GWh of battery materials per year.
Sila plans to expand the site in stages to a capacity of up to 250 GWh over the next five years. If completed as planned, the company says Moses Lake, Washington, would become the world’s largest anode production facility. The expansion is also expected to create several hundred additional jobs in Washington State.
The financing round was led by Atreides Management and Sutter Hill Ventures, with participation from existing and new investors including 8VC, Bessemer Venture Partners, Matrix Partners and funds advised by T. Rowe Price Associates.
“This funding allows us to further scale our operations and deliver on the promise of American innovation, strengthen the foundation of our domestic manufacturing capabilities and ensure that critical industries have a secure and reliable supply of advanced batteries,” said Gene Berdichevsky, Co-Founder and CEO of Sila.
Sila is a specialist in silicon anode material and plans to commercialise its proprietary product under the name ‘Titan Silicon’. As reported, the anode is said to be capable of offering 20 per cent higher energy density than today’s graphite cells. The innovative material is also expected to improve battery charging times. The battery material specialist’s first publicly announced automotive customer is
Mercedes-Benz
: the carmaker plans to use the Californian company’s silicon anode chemistry in the future electric G-Class.
Panasonic is also among the future customers
The announcement comes as the United States seeks to strengthen domestic battery supply chains. Sila argues that China’s dominant position in anode material processing and battery cell manufacturing creates strategic dependencies for industries including electric vehicles. The company positions domestic production of advanced battery materials as a way to improve supply chain resilience.
“Sila’s transition from a groundbreaking material science inventor to a gigascale manufacturing company is a masterclass in American operational discipline,” said Vic Miller of Sutter Hill Ventures. “We have watched them translate laboratory breakthroughs into reality, proving that the leap from the lab to the factory floor is not only essential, but achievable in the United States with the right technology and team.”

title: Banner powers into LFP market with ‘Traction Bull’ launch – Batteries International
content:
Lead, News, Other Chemistries
Banner powers into LFP market with ‘Traction Bull’ launch
–  July 22, 2026 11:00 am BST
Lead battery major Banner is venturing into the LFP market for the first time with the launch of the group’s Traction Bull Bloc Lithium product.
Austria-headquartered Banner said the newest addition to its portfolio, to be showcased at the Frankfurt Automechanika 2026 expo in September, is aimed at owners of motorhomes, travel trailers, boats, and stationary off-grid systems.
However, Banner said it remains true to its lead-powered roots and will also feature its Running Bull AGM and EFB batteries that have been developed specifically for vehicles with start-stop systems.
The new LFP series is being offered in five capacity levels and six battery types ranging from 50-150Ah.
Designed for deep-cycle applications, the new batteries achieve more than 3,000 cycles at an 80% DoD, Banner said. An integrated battery management system monitors cell voltages, current flow, and temperature, and protects against overcharging, deep discharge, short circuits, and overheating.
The 80Ah, 100Ah, and 150Ah models also feature a self-heating function, allowing the battery to be charged at temperatures as low as -20°C for year-round use outdoors.
Banner CEO Werner Töpfl said the new series responds to growing demand for high-performance, long-lasting, and smart energy storage solutions.
“Automechanika offers the ideal platform to present this innovation to an international audience of industry professionals for the first time.”
Batteries International
last month that Banner was investing a “significant double-digit million-euro amount” in its facilities in Austria over the next four years.
The move is part of the firm’s ‘Agenda 2035’ business programme, focusing on longer-term profitability, efficiency and growth beyond existing markets.
Photo: Banner

title: CATL, Alfen plan 5GWh sodium ion BESS parc across Europe – Batteries International
content:
Energy Storage, News, Other Chemistries
CATL, Alfen plan 5GWh sodium ion BESS parc across Europe
–  July 22, 2026 08:20 am BST
Plans to deploy 5GWh of sodium ion BESS systems in Europe have been unveiled by Chinese battery major CATL and Netherlands-based energy infrastructure specialist Alfen.
The companies said on July 16 the move marked the next step forward in a long-standing partnership that includes a battery supply deal agreed in 2023.
Initially focused on lithium ion energy storage projects, the supply partnership was extended in 2024 to support Alfen’s growing demand for ESS developments in Europe.
CATL said the joint deployment of sodium ion projects will allow it to gain experience in European grid applications for sodium batteries, optimising products for adaptation to European grid codes and accelerating the battery maker’s push to deploy its technology globally.
The partners did not give costings or timelines for their plans, but Tan Libin, CATL chief customer officer and co-president of sales and marketing, said the initiative establishes forward-looking cooperation on next-generation, low-cost energy storage solutions, and the opening of new business opportunities beyond traditional lithium ion battery storage supply.
Alfen CEO Michael Colijn said: “Sodium ion is the next step in how we think about energy storage — more diversified, resilient, and built for where the market is heading.”
Alfen, founded in 1937, has expanded from a focus on manufacturing switchgear and supplying secondary transformer substations to Dutch grid operators to embracing ESS and EV charging equipment in addition to smart grid tech.
Last February, CATL and Chinese auto firm Changan
what they said is the first mass-production car equipped with sodium ion batteries.

title: China tax freeze to boost new battery tech – Batteries International
content:
Lithium, News, Other Chemistries
China tax freeze to boost new battery tech
–  July 22, 2026 07:00 am BST
China's CATL launched its first generation sodium ion battery in 2021
China is introducing a temporary exemption from consumption tax for selected new-technology batteries for the next two years in a phased move to promote development of the sector.
Sodium ion and solid state are among a range of technologies that will be free from consumption tax from September this year to December 2028, under new rules issued by customs and tax authorities and the finance ministry.
The ruling, confirmed by state news agency Xinhua on July 17, takes effect on September 1 and is designed to promote sustainability and stimulate technological advancement.
Lithium ion, nickel-metal hydride and all vanadium redox flow batteries will be taxed at 2% from September and at 4% from September 2027. Photovoltaic cells will face a 2% tax from April 2027, rising to 4% from April 2028.
China currently levies a 4% consumption tax on batteries, a policy in place since February 2015, with seven categories including lithium ion and solar cells exempted, Xinhua said.
Consumption tax targets selected products deemed as luxury items or those with potential social or environmental impacts — unlike VAT, which is levied broadly on goods and services at various stages of the supply chain.
Batteries International
earlier this year that China was about to adjust or abolish export tax rebates for battery and photovoltaic products in response to volatility in international trade markets.
That came after China slapped a raft of export restrictions on technologies critical for manufacturing EV batteries in October 2025, in a move seen as a fresh bid to remind international markets that China had no intention of relinquishing dominance in the global batteries market for EVs and energy storage systems.

title: Range Rover GT Blends Luxury With EV Design
content:
The British automaker's fifth model blends grand touring design with EV architecture and a Scandinavian-inspired cabin.
July 22, 2026
The Range Rover GT brings a lower-slung model to the company's traditionally high-riding product family.
Jaguar Land Rover
The GT launches on a new EV platform with hybrid version coming later for the luxury segment.
Interior features synthetic leather, wool fabric, and panoramic skylight with minimal controls.
Physical buttons for trunk and hatch access avoid common EV design mistakes like Rivian's.
As carmakers increasingly shift their product lines toward SUVs, traditional SUV maker Range Rover has its eye on the car segment. The Range Rover brand has introduced the Range Rover GT,
a lower-slung car-like vehicle with a fastback roofline
that the company promises retains the off-road capability expected of Range Rover products.
The GT joins the product line that falls under the Range Rover brand since the reorganization of
’s various models. It will be Range Rover’s fifth model, alongside Range Rover,
Range Rover Sport
, Evoque, and Velar.
While only a camouflaged exterior has been revealed, the company showed the car’s interior in a reveal to journalists at the headquarters in Gaydon, UK. The GT launches as a battery-electric vehicle built on the company’s new platform, with a hybrid-electric version to follow later.
“The Range Rover GT will redefine the grand-touring segment with the ability to deliver beautiful and engaging GT poise, proportions, and long-distance refinement while offering capabilities no conventional GT can match,” promised Range Rover Managing Director Martin Limpert in the press announcement. “We have spent the last few years working obsessively on the fundamentals of the GT formula, reinterpreted in a sophisticated, uniquely Range Rover way.”
The GT prototype’s exterior is wrapped in a camouflage pattern that Range Rover says is a digital interpretation of the landscape contours surrounding the company’s offices, but the gold-toned pattern looks more like the scales of a reptilian snakeskin. In either case, the effect is cool, and the company would do well to consider offering the wrap as an option to customers looking for something more distinctive than the usual paint.
The battery pack in the Range Rover Sport provides a hint of what to expect in the GT. JAGUAR LAND ROVER
Range Rover says that the EMA EV architecture will deliver “first-class long-haul refinement and all-terrain capability,” even though it sits quite low to the ground in its normal stance and rolls on road-oriented tires. Land Rover vehicles commonly employ air suspension to provide higher ground clearance when needed, so that ability would be expected here.
The Range Rover EV’s cabin is the focus of this sneak preview, with an uncamouflaged look at the interior appointments. Seating surfaces are a perforated synthetic leather, while seatbacks are a wool fabric. There is a panoramic skylight in the GT’s roof, with cutouts in the tapered rear roof area to provide additional headroom over the rear seating positions for taller passengers.
It would be easy to imagine this minimalist dashboard design in a Volvo or Polestar. JAGUAR LAND ROVER
The dashboard features a large central infotainment screen, and there is a small instrument display ahead of the driver that is supplemented by a head-up display projected on the windshield.
There’s a single, hidden air vent running the width of the dashboard, so there are no conventional vent grates cluttering the clean design. There are also no visible dashboard buttons. There are four-way switches on both steering wheel spokes that will control functions that aren’t on the touch screen.
The GT has two physical buttons on the lower part of the dash to the left of the steering wheel on left-hand-drive models that provide an immediate way to open the front trunk and the rear hatch, avoiding one of the mistakes of the Rivian R1’s similarly screen-centric design that forces drivers to use the touch screen for these frequently accessed features.
Despite the low, sloping roofline, the Range Rover GT provides ample rear headroom. JAGUAR LAND ROVER
There are also physical window switches on the door panel, with none of the capacitive touch nonsense Volkswagen has tried to foist on customers.
These are the positive aspects of the design that Range Rover repeatedly describes as “reductive.” Land Rover, and by association, Range Rover, built its reputation on the old-world opulence of wood and leather surfaces. The Range Rover GT’s cabin has the austerity of Scandinavian design that you’d see in a Volvo or Polestar car, or even something like the aforementioned Rivian EVs.
It is understandable that Range Rover’s designers prefer to look to the future rather than to the past, but in the process, it is important that they don’t lose the things that have set British prestige brands apart from upstart pretenders to the luxury throne. The apparent lack of physical controls that may be necessary for the Chinese market (and hopefully others too) means that we could see some adjustments when the vehicle comes to market.
Whether that could include a bit more of the country club ambiance that has been the hallmark of Range Rover cabins remains to be seen.
Senior Editor, Design News
Dan’s coverage of the auto industry over three decades has taken him to the
, automotive engineering centers,
vehicle simulators
, wind tunnels, and crash-test labs of the world.
A member of the

contributes car reviews to

for automotive writing, as well as the

Driver of the Year award winner. Co-drove a Ford Focus 1.0-liter EcoBoost to
16 Federation Internationale de l’Automobile-accredited world speed records

title: Driving Innovation in Europe Battery Manufacturing with CATL
content:
Exclusive interview with CATL’s Feng Shen reveals insights on Europe’s battery industry growth, sustainable innovation, and future opportunities.
July 21, 2026
CATL's EV battery factory in Arnstadt, Germany.
Victor Golmer/iStock Editorial / Getty Images Plus
Feng (Matt) Shen, Managing Director of CATL Europe, understands the need for Europe to address critical challenges such as scaling up production, building resilient supply chains, and achieving cost-effective, sustainable manufacturing. In this exclusive interview with
Battery Technology
, Shen delves deeply into these topics, offering valuable perspectives on the future of Europe’s battery industry.
What do you see as the most significant barriers to scaling up battery production in Europe, and how can the industry collectively address these challenges to achieve cost-competitive, high-volume manufacturing?
Feng (Matt) Shen, Managing Director of CATL Europe:
Europe’s battery industry faces several interconnected challenges: high energy costs, fragmented supply chains, lengthy permitting processes, and a less mature industrial ecosystem compared with Asia.
A key constraint is the ability to translate industrial investment into stable, high-yield mass production. Battery manufacturing is a process industry where small inefficiencies in yield, material handling, or ramp-up learning curves have large cost implications at scale.
Building the Future of Battery Technology in Upstate New York
Another important factor is skills availability. Europe has strong automotive engineering capabilities, but large-scale battery manufacturing requires additional specialized know-how in electrochemistry, process engineering, and high-precision production operations.
To address these challenges, Europe needs a coordinated strategy across the entire value chain — including faster permitting, access to competitive energy, stronger supplier ecosystems, and targeted investment in industrial capability building.
Localization is also important. Building production close to customers and integrating regional supply chains can improve resilience, reduce logistics costs, and accelerate industrial learning. CATL’s investment in Germany, where it established the country’s only large-scale battery cell production facility of its kind, reflects this approach. Beyond manufacturing, the site also helps build industrial capability through engineering, quality, and training activities embedded in day-to-day operations.
Ultimately, Europe’s competitiveness will depend on combining industrial investment, advanced manufacturing, local supply chains, and continuous capability building within production environments.
How can Europe build a more robust and resilient battery supply chain, particularly in securing critical raw materials and components, to reduce reliance on imports and mitigate supply chain disruptions?
AI Could Strengthen Battery Traceability in EV Supply Chain Management
Europe needs a more diversified, localized, and circular battery supply chain. This means strengthening domestic refining and materials processing while building integrated ecosystems that connect raw materials, cell production, recycling, and automotive manufacturing.
Strategic partnerships across the value chain will be essential, particularly in areas such as cathode materials, precursor chemicals, and advanced manufacturing equipment. Europe already has strong industrial capabilities in chemicals, automation, and automotive engineering that can serve as a foundation for a more resilient battery ecosystem.
Recycling will be central to reducing import dependency. Recovering lithium, nickel, cobalt, and other materials from end-of-life batteries can significantly reduce exposure to global supply volatility. Industrial-scale recycling systems are already demonstrating recovery rates above 95% for key battery materials, showing the potential of closed-loop supply chains.
Localization also strengthens resilience. CATL’s European operations work with more than 1,000 European suppliers, helping support regional industrial ecosystems while shortening supply chains closer to customers. Partnerships across materials, automotive, and recycling industries are key to building a functioning circular value chain in Europe.
Why Conductive Additives Matter for Next-Generation Battery Cells
Europe’s goal should not be isolation from global trade, but rather diversified and balanced supply chains that are less vulnerable to geopolitical or logistical disruptions.
How can Europe strike a balance between achieving cost competitiveness in battery production and meeting sustainability goals, especially in the context of energy-intensive manufacturing processes?
Sustainability and competitiveness should reinforce each other. Low-carbon manufacturing is increasingly becoming a competitive advantage as OEMs, regulators, and consumers place greater emphasis on lifecycle emissions, traceability, and responsible sourcing.
Battery production is energy intensive, so access to affordable and reliable renewable electricity is critical. Europe can strengthen its position by accelerating renewable energy deployment and supporting industrial energy infrastructure near battery manufacturing hubs.
Advanced manufacturing, automation, and digitalization can also improve both sustainability and cost efficiency by increasing yields, reducing waste, and lowering energy consumption. In battery manufacturing, even small improvements in yield can have a significant impact on both cost and resource efficiency.
Localization contributes as well. Producing batteries closer to customers reduces transportation emissions and improves supply chain resilience. CATL’s European investments are based on this principle of producing where customers are and building regional supply chains around local manufacturing.
At the same time, circular economy solutions such as battery recycling, material recovery, and second-life applications will become increasingly important for reducing dependence on virgin raw materials and improving long-term sustainability.
Europe’s regulatory leadership — including the Battery Passport and broader traceability requirements — can also become a competitive advantage by establishing global standards for sustainable battery production.
In the long term, the companies and regions that successfully combine industrial scale with sustainability will likely be the most competitive globally.
What steps should Europe take to develop the skilled workforce needed to support the rapid scale-up of its battery industry, and how can collaboration between industry, academia, and governments play a role in this?
Europe’s challenge is not only to train more people, but to build a structured, long-term talent system for an entirely new industrial sector.
This requires stronger alignment between education systems and industrial demand. Universities alone are not sufficient; Europe also needs expanded vocational training, applied technical education, and structured pathways into manufacturing careers.
Industry plays a critical role by defining real production needs and providing hands-on learning environments. Academia contributes research capability and scientific depth, while governments enable scale through funding, curriculum reform, and recognition of new industrial profiles.
CATL Germany, for example, works closely with institutions such as Fraunhofer IKTS, TÜV Süd, and IHK to connect industrial practice with training frameworks. It also supports the development of dedicated vocational profiles such as a “Battery Professional” qualification, aimed at formalizing battery-specific expertise within Germany’s training system.
Over time, the goal is not just training individual workers, but building an integrated ecosystem of skills — from technicians and process engineers to quality specialists — capable of sustaining large-scale battery manufacturing in Europe.
CATL’s recycling operations achieve industry-leading recovery rates of up to 99.6% for nickel, cobalt, and manganese. Courtesy of Adobe Stock.
How can technological innovation and the adoption of circular economy principles, such as battery recycling and second-life applications, contribute to improving Europe’s competitiveness in the global battery market?
Technological innovation and circularity are central to CATL’s long-term approach and will be equally critical for Europe’s competitiveness. As a technology-driven company investing heavily in R&D and holding tens of thousands of patents globally, CATL has consistently focused on improving material efficiency, energy density, and lifecycle performance across battery systems.
One key area is recycling and closed-loop material recovery. CATL’s recycling operations achieve industry-leading recovery rates of up to 99.6% for nickel, cobalt, and manganese, and around 96.5% for lithium. The long-term ambition is to significantly reduce reliance on virgin raw materials through large-scale circular production systems.
Beyond recycling, second-life applications are becoming increasingly important. Battery systems originally designed for electric mobility are increasingly being repurposed for stationary energy storage, supporting grid stability and renewable integration. CATL already operates large-scale energy storage deployments globally, including utility and data-center applications, which demonstrate how battery technology can serve multiple sectors over its lifecycle.
Innovation is also accelerating cost and performance improvements. Technologies such as high-performance LFP systems, sodium-ion batteries, and ultra-fast charging architectures are expanding the application range of battery systems from mass-market EVs to heavy-duty transport and energy storage. This diversification strengthens resilience across the entire ecosystem.
Europe’s competitiveness will increasingly depend on combining this kind of technological innovation with industrial scale and circular material systems. The regions that can close the loop — from materials to production to reuse — while continuing to innovate on performance and safety will define the next phase of the global battery industry.
Maria Guerra
Senior Editor-Battery Technology, Informa Markets
Maria L. Guerra is a Senior Editor at
Battery Technology
, blending her engineering background with a passion for technology journalism. She holds a Bachelor of Science in Electrical Engineering from Universidad Metropolitana in Caracas, Venezuela, and a Master of Science in Electrical Engineering from NYU Tandon School of Engineering.
Guerra's career spans power systems engineering, oil & gas consulting, and technical writing and editing. She worked for several years at Kellogg, Brown & Root Ltd. (KBR) in the UK, conducting power system studies and providing technical support for offshore and onshore projects. During her internship at Pequiven S.A. in Venezuela, she trained neural networks using MATLAB (Kohonen Map) to estimate electrical circuit variables for oil wells (e.g., voltage, current, and power).
In addition to her engineering roles, Guerra worked as a freelance technical editor, refining complex technical content for clarity and accuracy. She later became a Power/Analog Editor at
Electronic Design
, covering advancements in the semiconductor market, focusing on power/analog electronics systems.
Battery Technology
, Guerra now delivers in-depth coverage of battery manufacturing, EV advancements, energy storage systems, and the evolving landscape of critical minerals and second-life batteries. She is passionate about uncovering the stories that shape the future of electrification, from cutting-edge battery innovations to policy shifts driving sustainable energy.
Guerra is also a member of
The Battery Show North America Advisory Board
, where she contributes her expertise to advising on industry trends and speaker selection for The Battery Show North America, while helping to shape discussions on battery technology and electrification. She was part of the award-winning Battery Technology team recognized with a
Neal Award for Best Coverage of the 2024 Election
Neal Award for Best Explanatory Content
in 2026 for her coverage of battery breakthroughs.
Maria Guerra is based in Brooklyn, NY, and can be reached at
[email protected]

title: Gartner: How Autonomous Tools are Reshaping Supply Chains
content:
Gartner: How Autonomous Tools are Reshaping Supply Chains
Prioritise Us
Gartner explores the leading supply chain technology trends for 2026, exploring robotics which are guiding operational transformation
Research firm Gartner has published eight technology trends that are reshaping
supply chain operations
The trends centre on AI systems spanning physical robotics and autonomous decision-making agents.
Gartner's analysis suggests that AI adoption in supply chains could enable organisations to build resilience while transforming operational models. The trends fall into three categories covering governance frameworks, intelligent systems and multiagent coordination.
According to Gartner, the identified trends point towards systems that can operate independently across digital and physical supply chain environments. These technologies are allowing supply chains to function with less human intervention in routine decision-making.
Christian Titze, VP Analyst and Chief of Research in Gartner's Supply Chain practice, says: "This year's trends highlight the growing role of AI as the foundation for
more autonomous
, intelligent and adaptive supply chains.
"As organisations move towards hyperconnected, AI-driven environments, leaders must focus not only on deploying advanced technologies but also on ensuring they work together to deliver measurable value and long-term resilience."
The research identifies multiple AI architectures that could address workforce constraints while improving operational flexibility. Each technology addresses specific supply chain challenges from labour shortages to real-time execution needs.
These trends represent more than incremental improvements. They are catalysts for transforming supply chains.
Christian Titze, VP Analyst and Chief of Research in Gartner’s Supply Chain practice
Gartner highlighted polyfunctional robots as a technology that could address labour gaps in supply chain operations. These machines can perform multiple tasks within a single system rather than requiring dedicated equipment for each function.
The firm also identified physical AI as a top trend for 2026. Physical AI combines AI models with
, robotics and automation systems to enable real-time sensing, analysis and execution across supply chain environments.
Agentic AI appeared in Gartner's trend list as a technology capable of planning, acting and adapting to achieve goals in complex environments. These systems could execute supply chain decisions without requiring constant human oversight.
Collaborative multiagent systems enable multiple
to work together across workflows and environments. According to Gartner, organisations could use these systems to automate complex, multistep processes while improving scalability and adaptability.
A BCG survey found that 44% of companies are deploying AI in supply chain management
Gartner estimates 50% of cross-functional supply chain management solutions will use intelligent agents to autonomously execute decisions by 2030
Gartner identified collaborative multiagent systems, which enable multiple AI agents to work together, in its supply chain technology trends for 2026
According to The Boston Consulting Group, 44% of companies are
deploying AI
in supply chain management. The BCG survey suggests that AI adoption in supply chain contexts has reached substantial levels across organisations.
Christian says: "These trends represent more than incremental improvements. They are catalysts for transforming supply chains. Organisations that proactively evaluate and integrate these technologies in line with their business objectives will be better positioned to navigate disruption, scale innovation and maintain competitive advantage."
Gartner has previously forecast that by 2030, 50% of cross-functional supply chain management solutions will use intelligent agents to autonomously execute decisions in the ecosystem.
Gartner identified intelligent simulation as a trend that integrates AI, machine learning and advanced analytics into simulation models. This technology enables more dynamic planning across logistics, transportation and warehouse operations.
The firm also highlighted decision governance as organisations scale their AI adoption. Companies are implementing frameworks and guardrails to govern AI-enabled decision-making, ensuring transparency, accountability and compliance.
Domain-specific language models appeared in the trend list as AI systems that can be fine-tuned for
supply chain applications
. These models could understand supply chain terminology and processes better than general-purpose AI systems.
Gartner identified product provenance as a technology to trace and verify the origin and journey of products across the supply chain. This capability could address transparency requirements and regulatory compliance needs in global supply networks.
VP Analyst and Chief of Research, Supply Chain

title: Global Aid: How Logistics Firms are Responding to Venezuela
content:
Global Aid: How Logistics Firms are Responding to Venezuela
Prioritise Us
Following the June 24 earthquakes in Venezuela, logistics giants around the world are using their global networks to bring critical aid to the country
On 24 June 2026, two
earthquakes hit Venezuela
, measuring at 7.2 and 7.5 magnitude. The damage has cut off communications and power in many areas, with destruction across the nation – in the northern state of La Guaira, 80% of buildings collapsed as a result.
More than 4,500 people are dead, with thousands more displaced or injured.
Now, logistics companies around the world are using their global networks to provide support to those in need.
Getting critical supply to disaster areas takes a significant amount of planning and logistics know-how. It requires rapid and coordinated mobilisation of goods and people, often relying on a pre-positioned relief supplies and public-private partnerships.
Goods need to be stored in strategic areas – close to high-risk regions but not close enough that they are at risk of the impact. Close collaboration between government organisations, NGOs and private companies is critical, helping distribute resources and time support accurately to avoid delays or condensed support.
Access to affected areas is a major challenge in delivering support, as road and infrastructure are often damaged. This requires flexible logistics solutions, multimodal transportation options and flexibility to adapt. Boats, off-road vehicles and helicopters are critical for delivering aid amid natural disasters.
Once there, managing inventory is another significant challenge, with the need to monitor resources accurately to
ensure efficiency
. Moreover, urgent resources need to be handed out first, with a fair distribution focus to avoid mobs, as well as a way to tide over citizens until more resources can arrive.
Global logistics giants have begun using their inventory management and distribution capabilities, as well as their multimodal expertise in order to support across Venezuela.
Amazon has been selected as the corporate partner for the launching of a humanitarian air bridge, making use of Amazon Air Cargo. This makes use of seven weekly flights going into Caracas, the capital of Venezuela.
Through collaboration between Amazon, Amazon Air Cargo, the US State Department, Airlink and the UN World Food Programme, this public-private partnership model will be using each organisation's individual strengths and resources to deliver aid in a cohesive way.
“When more than six million people need help, you look at what you've built and put it to work. We have one of the largest freight operations in the industry — built for reliability, built for scale," explains Peter Larsen, Vice President of Amazon Supply Chain Services.
"Amazon Air Cargo is now tapping its network to deliver emergency supplies into Caracas, seven flights a week, at no cost to the humanitarian organisations on the ground. Same logistics network, same aviation capabilities we use every day to move cargo and packages — deployed to help the people who need it most."
This model relies on:
The collaborative scale is critical to ensure distribution of necessary goods is streamlined, reaching as many people as possible. Through ongoing communication and expansive logistics capabilities, the organisations are aiming to prevent aid
bottlenecks and avoid
wrongful allocations.
"This collaboration with Amazon, Airlink and World Food Programme demonstrates what's possible when government and the private sector come together to overcome logistical barriers in a humanitarian crisis. By facilitating access and coordinating with local authorities, we're helping ensure that lifesaving aid reaches Venezuela as quickly as possible," explains Tommy Pigott, US State Department Spokesperson.
is also offering relief to Venezuela, using its integrated air and ground network to support those affected. It has a dedicated humanitarian relief flight which transported kits provided by Global Empowerment Mission (GEM).
The shipment, landing in Caracas on July 19, is expected to support 10,000 people. Through collaboration with GEM, it has delivered water, food and hygiene products to communities in La Guaria.
Richard W. Smith, Chief Operating Officer, International, and Chief Executive Officer, Airline at FedEx, says: “Our unmatched air and ground global network gives FedEx the unique ability to respond when critical supplies need to reach communities in crisis. The tireless dedication and united effort of our team members are what make a humanitarian mission like this possible. We are proud to help GEM deliver essential supplies to the people of Venezuela.”
Other collaborative measures have taken place across the logistics industry in order to support Venezuela;
– through Air Cargo Hub Punta Cana – has formed a partnership with UniWorld Air Cargo, Grupo Velutini and Athiopica. This collaborative effort is delivering 27 tons of humanitarian aid to communities, using two flights to carry supplies from Punta Cana to Caracas.
The operation was coordinated by the logistics platform Air Cargo Hub Punta Cana, which was developed by DP World and Grupo Puntacana. The platform also ensured logistics management, cargo processing and operational support, whereas Grupo Velutini and Athiopica managed the collection, sorting and consolidation of goods. The aircraft capacity was provided by UniWorld Air Cargo.
"At DP World, we believe logistics is about more than moving cargo — it is about connecting people and supporting communities, particularly when it matters most," says Manuel Martínez, CEO of DP World's operations in the Dominican Republic.
"By working alongside our partners, we were able to leverage our infrastructure and operational expertise to help deliver critical
humanitarian supplies
quickly and efficiently to those affected by this disaster. This initiative reflects the power of collaboration across business sectors."
Through industry collaboration and logistics expertise from all parts of a project, organisations from around the world are working together to support relief in Venezuela. Logistics companies are navigating complexity, particularly regarding cargo processing and allocation, to ensure streamlined distribution of critical goods.
Logistics projects of this scale are immense, requiring significant planning, coordination and cooperation. These giants, and more, are demonstrating how supply chain expertise can be used to support communities in need.
CEO DP World Dominican Republic
Vice President of Amazon Multichannel Commerce & Fulfilment

title: Europcar: UK Supply Chains at Risk of Sustainability Reports
content:
Europcar: UK Supply Chains at Risk of Sustainability Reports
Prioritise Us
As supply chains around the world face pressure to comply to sustainability needs, many businesses are facing major barriers to sustainable upgrades
In February 2026, finalised versions of the UK SRS S and UK SRS S2 were published, with a policy statement expected in Autumn 2026.
With this, organisations are needing to make significant changes to
ensure compliance
Europcar Mobility Group explores the risk to businesses who cannot upgrade in time.
Many corporates are already reporting their sustainability-related information voluntarily, acting early to reduce emissions.
Tom Middleditch, Head of B2B Marketing at Europcar Mobility Group UK
The new requirements mean there will be a baseline, which will drive improved levels of consistency and transparency across operations. As a result, it will also mean stronger links between sustainability reporting and risk reporting.
The UK SRS acts as the UK's framework for
corporate reporting
on sustainability and climate-related risks. The risks focus on areas of business operations, but mobility comes under focus as a main cause of emissions. To avoid scrutiny and losing contracts, businesses need to integrate low- and zero-emissions vehicles into their fleets.
This, however, is an expensive change to make. Though statistics show lower costs over time, the initial expense of changing entire fleets is something many organisations cannot afford.
“Switching a fleet to fully electric in a short space of time is impractical for most businesses operating in the supply chain ecosystem,” explains Tom Middleditch, Head of B2B Marketing and Sustainability spokesperson at Europcar Mobility Group UK.
“And current economic conditions make significant multi-year financial commitments challenging, meaning long-term leasing of
electric cars and vans
isn’t practical or possible for many organisations. However, rental can provide a viable alternative – and help ensure that contracts are not at risk as the UK SRS comes into force.”
Main innovations for sustainable supply chains include
Adoption of low and zero-carbon fuels
Use of electric or hydrogen trucks
Building sustainable warehousing
Use of AI to improve route optimisation and fuel efficiency
Multimodal capabilities offering efficiency and reduced carbon intensity
Ongoing improvements to last-mile delivery
Europcar argues that rental offers a low-risk means of EV adoption, as it is flexible and a much smaller commitment. As organisations can opt to rent a series of EV vehicles instead of outright buying them, they can meet the sustainability measures of customers while also balancing costs.
As no purchasing or making is required to meet
fleet demands
, it is an immediate and scalable option. Due to its lack of permanence, it also allows businesses to trial the various types of EV in order to make long-term informed decisions.
Supply chains therefore can experience the pros and cons of electric mobility options over a long time period, without having made a conclusive decision before understanding how each vehicle operates within its organisation.
“Many corporates are already reporting their sustainability-related information voluntarily, acting early to reduce emissions, while others are planning for January,” Tom adds.
“These listed businesses will already be reviewing and switching partners based on their sustainability credentials, so suppliers must act quickly to ensure they do not lose existing contracts or miss out on new opportunities."
Organisations like Europcar are supporting the transition to
electric fleets
, helping drivers become familiar with EVs without the high expense and commitment.
As the energy transition takes place, logistics organisations and supply chain businesses need to examine their
emissions reduction strategies
. For some, this may focus on changing suppliers or implementing circularity, but for others, it will focus on more sustainable logistics options.
Those looking to make immediate changes to their operations – but do not have the funds or confidence in a full fleet transition – can find short-term solutions which can transform their logistics and meet consumer demand for emissions reductions.
Head of B2B Marketing UK & Ireland

title: This country went 100% electric vehicles overnight with a drastic approach
content:
Fred Lambert
| Jul 22 2026 - 7:30 am PT
Laos did something no other country has tried: it banned the import of new gas and diesel passenger cars outright. As of June 1, nearly every new car cleared for import has to be electric.
It’s the most aggressive EV mandate on the planet right now, and it has almost nothing to do with consumer demand.
The Lao government
suspended imports of petrol- and diesel-powered vehicles
effective June 1, 2026, with the ban running through the end of the year. The Ministry of Industry and Commerce is handling implementation. With combustion imports frozen, the only new cars legally entering the country are electric. Hence the “100%” electric import figure in June.
But there’s a catch. Actually, several.
The ban isn’t total. Public transport vehicles, construction machinery, project-related trucks, and other specialized vehicles are exempt. Diesel keeps flowing where the country can’t yet electrify. This is a passenger-car policy, aimed squarely at the segment where affordable electric alternatives already exist.
Laos isn’t doing this for climate headlines. The logic is economic.
The country runs almost entirely on hydropower and has built its export strategy around selling electricity to its neighbors. But it imports every drop of the fuel its cars burn, and that fuel bill is paid in foreign currency Laos is chronically short on. Every gas car swapped for an electric one runs on domestic hydro instead of imported diesel. That’s the whole game.
To push buyers toward EVs, Laos is pairing the stick with carrots. Fully electric vehicles priced under $50,000 get a full excise tax exemption. The government cut EV registration fees. And it’s requiring transportation companies to run at least 10% electric fleets by the end of 2026.
There’s an infrastructure push behind it too. In April, Laos signed an agreement with 27 public and private partners to build out charging stations, battery-swapping stations, a central digital platform, and financing products. The national target is 30% of vehicles electric by 2030.
The space left by combustion cars is being filled almost entirely by China. As
we reported in June
, Chinese EV exports to ASEAN hit $1.2 billion in a single month, with Laos posting record volumes alongside Cambodia. Chinese brands and Vietnam’s VinFast, through its Xanh SM taxi platform, already have footholds in the market.
A ban on gas imports is, in practice, a subsidy for whoever sells cheap EVs — and right now that’s Chinese automakers.
Laos is an outlier, but the direction isn’t. Global EV sales
hit 2 million in June
, Europe just posted a record battery-electric month, and China’s electric heavy trucks crossed 50% penetration. Cambodia scrapped customs duties on EVs earlier this year. Across Southeast Asia, governments are treating electrification as energy security, not a green talking point.
The headline writes itself. A country went nearly 100% EV overnight. But the mechanism matters, and it’s worth being clear-eyed about it.
Laos didn’t win over car buyers. It removed the alternative. That’s a very different thing from what’s happening in Norway or even China, where EVs are winning on price, performance, and choice. A ban works on paper, but it only holds if people can actually buy and charge the electric cars that are left. In a country where charging is thin and incomes are low, that’s a real question, and the exemptions for trucks and machinery show the government knows it can’t flip everything at once.
Still, don’t dismiss it. This is what energy-security-driven electrification looks like when a country has cheap domestic power and an expensive fuel-import bill. Laos is a small market, but it’s a preview of the math a lot of oil-importing nations are about to run. When your electricity is homegrown hydro and your gasoline is bought in scarce dollars, banning the gas car stops being radical and starts looking like arithmetic.
The open question is whether the charging network and the cars can keep the promise this decree just made. We’ll be watching the back half of 2026 to find out.

title: Range Rover's next EV is unlike anything the luxury brand has built before [Images]
content:
Peter Johnson
| Jul 22 2026 - 7:26 am PT
revealed the first images of the all-electric GT, a stunning new grand tourer built for the long haul. The interior is even more of a surprise.
While Range Rover is gearing up to launch
its flagship electric SUV
later this year, the British luxury brand announced plans to add a fifth member to its lineup.
Range Rover confirmed on Wednesday that the new GT will be “the most technologically sophisticated Range Rover ever created,” promising it will redefine the grand-touring segment.
Swaying away from the boxy SUVs the brand has built its reputation on, the electric GT sits much lower to the ground with a sporty, coupe-like silhouette.
The meticulous design comes after Range Rover spent the “last few years working obsessively on the fundamentals of the GT formula,” according to Martin Limbert, the brand’s managing director.
“The result is the most car‑like, yet unmistakably capable, Range Rover ever created,” Limbert added, confirming it will be the first all-electric Range Rover to use Jaguar Land Rover’s new EMA platform.
The new EMA platform was initially designed as a dedicated EV architecture, but JLR recently said it plans to release new hybrids on it as well.
Range Rover also previewed the interior, which features a surprisingly minimalist design. “The interior of Range Rover GT is shaped by the same reductive principles that define its exterior proportions: purity, precision and quiet modernity,” Limbert explained.
A single screen now sits at the center with a slim driver display that replaces the traditional instrument cluster.
Surprisingly, there are no physical buttons, which many automakers like Volkswagen have begun to reintroduce into their latest EVs.
Range Rover will reveal more details, including battery specs, later this year, with prototypes now in the final stages of testing.
The GT “will redefine the grand‑touring segment with the ability to deliver beautiful and engaging GT poise, proportions and long‑distance refinement,” according to Limbert.
Range Rover’s flagship electric SUV, based on the MLA platform, will use a 118 kWh battery pack, providing a range of 373 miles (600 km). The GT is expected to use a 100+ kWh battery pack, which could enable close to 500 miles (800 km) of range with its slippery fastback design.
The Range Rover GT will be built at JLR’s Halewood, UK plant. It will initially go on sale as a pure electric vehicle, but Range Rover plans to add a full hybrid variant “later in the lifecycle.”
Production is set to begin in the first half of 2027, following the flagship Range Rover Electric and smaller Sport models.
Prices have to be confirmed, but Range Rover’s flagship EV is expected to start at just under £150,000 ($200,900) in the UK. Positioned below the Velar, the Range Rover GT will likely be over £75,000 ($100,000).
In the US, the electric Range Rover GT is expected to start closer to $90,000 with an EPA range of at least 300 miles on a single charge.
For those who prefer the traditional SUV look, the
Range Rover Sport EV
will go on sale shortly after the flagship model, offering a more affordable electric option.

title: Exclusive: Segway teases more electric bikes, including a street-legal next-gen Xyber
content:
| Jul 22 2026 - 6:00 am PT
Segway has made major strides in the e-bike space recently, but the company isn’t done expanding its electric bicycle lineup just yet.
Following the launches of the Myon commuter e-bike and MUXI cargo bike
earlier this year
, the company has confirmed to
that it plans to unveil two additional e-bike models before the end of 2026, with both expected to become available in the third quarter.
is keeping most of the details under wraps for now, the company did share with us one significant teaser: one of the upcoming models will introduce the next-generation Xyber platform.
Unlike the current Xyber, which
blurs the line
between e-bike and lightweight electric motorcycle, the new version is being engineered as a street-legal e-bike with selectable Class 1, Class 2, and Class 3 operation, where permitted by applicable laws. That should make it far more accessible to riders looking for the Xyber’s futuristic styling and technology in a package that fits within established e-bike regulations.
According to Segway, the new platform will build on the original Xyber’s distinctive design while adding greater everyday practicality. The company says riders can expect a combination of performance-focused engineering, connected technology, and rider-adjustable functionality, though specifications, pricing, and final availability are still being kept under wraps.
“The next generation of the Xyber platform reflects how we are continuing to evolve our e-bike
technology,” said Heather Henderson, Segway’s Senior Brand Manager. “It builds on the bold character of the original model while introducing a street-legal option designed to deliver performance, intelligent features, and greater everyday versatility.”
Segway also confirmed that a second all-new e-bike is on the way, though the company isn’t sharing any details about that model just yet.
“We’re excited by the momentum behind Segway’s e-bike business,” said George Lee, Sales Operations Director at Segway. “With two additional e-bike launches planned for 2026, we are continuing to broaden our portfolio and serve a wider range of riders across the U.S. market.”
The announcement continues Segway’s aggressive expansion into the North American e-bike market.
After first making headlines
with the high-performance Xyber and Xafari models, the company has rapidly broadened its lineup this year with the
Myon commuter series and the MUXI short-tail cargo bike.
We’ll have more details on both upcoming models as soon as Segway is ready to officially pull the covers off later this year.

title: Electric, autonomous, and there's 800 of them: inside this week's BIGGEST deal
content:
| Jul 22 2026 - 5:27 am PT
In a massive deployment of self-driving trucks that makes most autonomous and electric vehicle deployments look tiny in comparison, Chinese heavy equipment brand Tonly just signed a deal to deliver 800 autonomous, “New Energy” mining trucks to Xinjiang Hanxiang.
While Tesla’s heavily-hyped Robotaxi fleet stalls at 17 units in Austin and a handful more roll out in Tampa and Orlando
, executives from Tonly took to the stage at the brand’s Tonly Heavy Industries Xinjiang Regional Green Intelligence Mine Innovation and Development Seminar in Ürümqi to announce that it would be delivering 800 autonomous mining trucks to mining firm Xinjiang Hanxiang.
As the largest city of the
Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region
in Northwest China, the location was fitting – and the seminar also saw Tonly and its partners in the green smart mine construction industry chain also jointly signed a Green Smart Mine Ecological Alliance strategy, according to a report from
International Mining
“Tonly already has fleets of new energy and autonomous trucks running at many of the major Xinjiang coal mining operations including those owned by TBEA, Guanghui Energy, Xinjiang Yihua and others – where in most cases the customer is a large contract mining group,”
. “Key new energy models include the Tonly TLE135 90 t payload battery electric truck which can support either high-speed plug-in charging or rapid battery-swapping technology.”
The 800 trucks set to be delivered to Xinjiang will join Tonly’s global fleet of electric mining trucks,
which includes 50 examples of the 90-ton DTE145 battery electric haul truck deployed by Grupo Minero Zijin in Peru
Tonly’s DTE145 is a massive,
90-tonne-class rigid haul truck fitted with a 770 kWh swappable battery pack
(up to 1,200 kWh is available) from CATL that sends power to a 620 kW (~830 hp) electric drivetrain. The DTE145 is designed around a battery-swap system that can replace a depleted pack in about seven minutes, making it ideal for quick driver swaps between shifts.
At the Ürümqi autonomous site, though,
that just means the big EVs will experience seven minutes of downtime
– but because there’s no “fuel tank swap” technology, that seven minute swap time means these big trucks will ready to get back to work again in
time than their diesel-powered competition.
With reduced maintenance and repair bills,
dramatically lower fuel costs
, and the added safety of proven autonomous technology these vehicles can deliver, it’s no wonder that major mining companies are adopting electric vehicles at a record pace. We love to see it!
SOURCES | IMAGES
International Mining

title: Caltech’s New Device Steers Light With Light at Mind-Bending Speed
content:
A light-controlled meta-surface redirected another beam at femtosecond speeds without relying on slower electronic relaxation.
A pulse of light crosses the width of a human hair in roughly 74 femtoseconds (74 quadrillionths of a second). Caltech researchers have now redirected a beam in that same fleeting interval by using a second beam as the control signal.
The achievement points toward photonic technologies that move and process information with light rather than relying entirely on electrical currents. Such systems could support faster communications, more powerful computing, and more sensitive sensors. Reaching that potential, however, requires components capable of steering light with comparable speed and precision.
“Steering light with light is very challenging because light typically interacts very weakly with matter. Using optical meta-surfaces (ultrathin, carefully nanoengineered sheets), we can up the interaction strength to make this possible with much higher efficiency,” says Harry Atwater, the Howard Hughes Professor of Applied Physics and Materials Science and the Otis Booth Leadership Chair of the Division of Engineering and Applied Science at Caltech.
The work was reported in
Nature Nanotechnology
. Lead author Claudio Hail conducted the research while working as a postdoctoral scholar in Atwater’s Caltech laboratory. He is now an assistant professor of mechanical engineering at UC Berkeley.
Existing devices often steer or modulate light by changing the electronic behavior of a material. Liquid crystal projector panels and telecommunications chips, for example, alter how light travels by pushing electrons into higher energy states.
Those electrons must eventually return to lower energy states and release the energy they absorbed. This recovery period creates a built-in delay, typically limiting modulation to nanoseconds or picoseconds (trillionths of a second).
Atwater’s group looked for a way around that bottleneck. Instead of controlling the material with electricity, the researchers used an intense patterned beam called the pump. Its purpose was to briefly alter the optical behavior of the material.
A second, weaker beam called the probe then traveled through the same material. The pattern carried by the pump determined the direction in which the probe was deflected, allowing light itself to perform the steering.
The method relies on the optical Kerr effect. During this phenomenon, an intense beam produces a tiny and temporary change in a material’s refractive index, which describes how much light slows and bends while moving through that substance.
The pump creates this change by slightly shifting the motion of electrons
their orbitals, the regions surrounding an atomic nucleus where electrons are most likely to be found. The electrons do not move into separate, longer-lived energy states. As a result, the change begins and ends almost as quickly as the light pulse, removing the need to wait for electronic relaxation.
That speed solves one problem but introduces another. By itself, the optical Kerr effect is too weak to bend a beam far enough for many practical uses.
To strengthen it, the researchers fashioned a thin layer of amorphous silicon into a meta-surface covered with
pillars. Each pillar was smaller than the wavelength of the pump light.
The dimensions and spacing of the pillars were chosen so that light would briefly circulate within the structure instead of passing through immediately. Keeping the light inside for slightly longer amplified the small change in silicon’s refractive index until it became strong enough to redirect the probe beam.
Using this design, the researchers steered light by as much as 13 degrees in only 74 femtoseconds. They also found that the modulation speed matched the duration of the pump pulse, which lasted 74 femtoseconds.
The device itself does not yet appear to impose the main speed limit. Instead, performance is currently constrained by the duration of the laser pulses used to operate it.
Shorter pulses could therefore push the switching time even lower. Further development may move the system into the speed range needed to explore emerging photonic ideas, including time crystals and synthetic time-varying optical materials.
Reference: “Ultrafast, reconfigurable all-optical beam steering and spatial light modulation” by Claudio U. Hail, Lior Michaeli and Harry A. Atwater, 22 June 2026,
Nature Nanotechnology
DOI: 10.1038/s41565-026-02199-w

title: Quantum Gravity May Be Far Less Quantum Than Physicists Expected
content:
A new study reveals that some proposed signatures of quantum gravity may have an unexpected classical explanation.
Imagine placing an object in two locations at once. Quantum mechanics allows such a possibility, at least for sufficiently small systems. But if the object occupies multiple positions, what happens to the gravity surrounding it?
That question lies at the center of one of physics’ most persistent problems. Quantum mechanics describes atoms, particles, and other microscopic systems with remarkable
, while Einstein’s theory of gravity explains planets, stars, black holes, and the expansion of the Universe. Physicists still lack a single theory of “quantum gravity” that can describe both realms together.
Quantum mechanics allows an object to become delocalized, meaning its possible location is spread across more than one place. Experiments have demonstrated this behavior with atoms and even small pieces of metal. Einstein’s theory, meanwhile, treats gravity as the shape of space and time itself. Spacetime can curve, remain flat, or carry waves, as gravitational wave detectors have confirmed.
If an object can occupy several quantum states at once, many physicists expect the surrounding spacetime to do the same. The difficulty is determining what evidence would distinguish that possibility from ordinary gravity acting on a quantum object.
But what would such a situation actually look like?
Researchers from Kyushu University, the
University of Waterloo
, and Stockholm University addressed this problem by examining how proposed quantum gravity experiments can be interpreted. Their work, published in
npj Quantum Information
, shows that some apparently quantum forms of gravity may have an alternative description.
They developed a theoretical framework showing that many situations labeled a “quantum superposition of gravity” can also be understood as quantum particles occupying superpositions while moving through ordinary gravity and spacetime. Under that second description, the particles behave quantum mechanically, but gravity itself does not need to display an observable quantum signature.
This equivalence creates a challenge for experiments. A result that appears to show gravity occupying several states might also emerge from familiar spacetime combined with the quantum behavior of matter.
“Many researchers have proposed experiments that could potentially reveal the quantum nature of gravity,” explains Associate Professor Joshua Foo of Kyushu University’s Institute for Advanced Study and lead author of the study. “What we found is that some of these scenarios can be viewed from two equally valid perspectives. One interpretation describes gravity as being in a quantum superposition, while the other describes quantum particles moving in an ordinary gravitational field.”
The researchers call the concept the “Relativity of Spacetime Superpositions.” The central idea is that the same physical situation can sometimes be represented in two different ways, much as different map projections can depict the same landscape.
That does not establish that gravity is classical, and it does not argue against quantum gravity. Instead, it exposes a problem of interpretation. An experiment may produce a result consistent with quantum gravity without showing that a quantum description of gravity is the only possible explanation.
“Our work does not tell us that such experiments rule out quantum gravity,” says Magdalena Zych of Stockholm University and a co-author on the paper. “Rather, it helps us identify which experimental signatures would genuinely require a quantum description of gravity and which ones could arise from more familiar physics. That distinction is crucial for designing future experiments.”
The framework gives physicists a clearer standard for evaluating proposed tests. Rather than asking only whether a result can be described using quantum gravity, researchers must determine whether ordinary gravity combined with quantum matter could produce the same observation.
That distinction can help narrow the experimental search. Measurements that remain explainable through classical spacetime cannot provide unambiguous evidence that gravity itself follows quantum rules. More decisive tests will need to isolate signatures that no classical description can reproduce.
The immediate goal is theoretical, but research into fundamental physics has often produced practical consequences that were not apparent at the outset.
navigation depends on Einstein’s theory of gravity, while lasers and modern electronics grew from advances in quantum physics.
For now, the framework serves as a guide for designing and interpreting experiments aimed at one of modern physics’ most difficult questions. By identifying where classical and quantum explanations overlap, it helps researchers focus on observations that could genuinely separate them.
“Understanding how gravity and quantum mechanics fit together is one of the greatest challenges in physics,” concludes Foo. “Before we can test gravity’s quantum nature, we first need to know what evidence would prove that we’ve found it. Our work helps clarify that question.”
Reference: “Relativity and decoherence of spacetime superpositions” by Joshua Foo, Cendikiawan Suryaatmadja, Robert B. Mann and Magdalena Zych, 13 May 2026,
npj Quantum Information
DOI: 10.1038/s41534-026-01234-x

title: Scientists Discover Why Teenage Memories Can Become Temporarily Inaccessible
content:
Some memories from adolescence may not disappear. The brain may simply lock them away for a time while its memory system is being rebuilt.
A study in mice has uncovered an unexpected wave of remodeling in a brain region that helps preserve and retrieve long-term memories. During late adolescence, the protective structures surrounding key neurons temporarily weakened, making experiences from earlier life harder to recall. Those memories often returned in adulthood, but some had lost their original precision.
The findings, published in
PLOS Biology
The researchers focused on the retrosplenial cortex (RSP), an area that helps connect experiences with places, context, and previously stored information. Although it receives less public attention than the hippocampus, the RSP is an important part of the network the brain uses to retrieve established memories.
In late adolescent mice, the team found that perineuronal nets in the RSP declined sharply before recovering in adulthood. These mesh-like structures form around certain neurons and help stabilize the connections that preserve information over time. Their temporary loss may make memory circuits more flexible, but it may also make older memories more difficult to access.
The same changes did not occur in the nearby hippocampus, which is strongly involved in forming and organizing new memories. That regional difference suggests that adolescence does not reshape every part of the memory system in the same way.
“We’ve known for years that the brain continues developing through adolescence and young adulthood,” said senior author Jelena Radulovic, M.D., Ph.D., professor in the Dominick P. Purpura Department of Neuroscience and of psychiatry and behavioral sciences at Einstein.
“Our findings begin to explain what that developmental process looks like in one of the brain’s memory circuits and how it can influence the way earlier experiences are recalled.
“We do not yet fully understand the consequences of the observed fluctuations of perineuronal nets, but we believe that their reorganization in RSP helps prioritize access to memories formed in adulthood at the expense of those formed in early adolescence. This could help to better adapt to the circumstances and challenges encountered during adult life stages.
“We are investigating whether preventing such fluctuations in adolescence would have any negative consequences in later life.”
Earlier studies had suggested that the memory circuits examined by the team were largely mature by early adolescence. The new results challenge that view. Rather than remaining stable, an important part of the system appears to enter a temporary period of instability later in adolescence before settling again in adulthood.
That timing is especially relevant because the human brain also continues developing well beyond the teenage years. Processes involved in planning, impulse control, emotional regulation, and decision-making can continue maturing into the mid-to-late 20s. According to the
National Institutes of Health
, the brain remains underdeveloped during this extended period.
“The behavior matched the biology,” said lead author Hui Zhang, Ph.D., a research fellow at Einstein. “The retrosplenial cortex is responsible for older, more established memories. As its stabilizing structures declined, access to memories formed earlier in life became less reliable.”
To test the effects on memory, the researchers placed mice in a chamber where they received a mild foot shock. When returned shortly afterward, the animals froze, showing that they remembered the unpleasant experience.
The response changed with time. Many mice trained during early adolescence stopped freezing when they were returned to the chamber several weeks later. Adult mice trained in the same way continued to respond, indicating that their memories remained more stable.
The adolescent memories had not been erased. When the mice were reminded of the unpleasant event in a different setting, their fear of the original chamber returned. The experiment showed that the information was still stored but had become temporarily difficult to retrieve.
The researchers linked this loss of access to reduced levels of proteins needed to build and maintain perineuronal nets. Activity of TGFβ2, a growth factor that supports those structures, also fell during the same period.
When the team strengthened the nets or restored TGFβ2 activity, the animals again became able to retrieve memories formed earlier in life. That result provided direct evidence that the structural changes were not merely associated with memory loss but were helping cause the retrieval problem.
Such generalization may reflect a tradeoff. Remembering the broad emotional lesson of an experience can be useful even when its precise context has faded. A dangerous encounter, for example, may shape future behavior even if the details of where and when it happened become blurred.
The researchers compared this pattern with the “reminiscence bump,” the tendency for adults to recall an unusually large number of memories from adolescence and early adulthood. Those memories often retain strong emotional meaning even when their finer details are incomplete.
Whether the two phenomena are driven by the same biology remains unknown. The researchers say the later return of adolescent memories could result from an age-related increase in perineuronal nets, repeated exposure to similar experiences, the replay of old events, or another process that has yet to be identified.
Schizophrenia and major depression frequently emerge during late adolescence, which overlaps with the period of extensive circuit remodeling observed in the mice.
The researchers suggest that this temporary instability may create a sensitive developmental window. In people with a genetic susceptibility, disruptions to the process could potentially increase the risk of psychiatric illness. However, the experiments were conducted in mice, and further research will be needed to establish whether human memory circuits undergo the same changes.
Reference: “Retrosplenial cortical reorganization during late adolescence introduces instability of contextual memory circuits” by Hui Zhang, Zorica Petrovic, Elizabeth M. Wood, Ana Cicvaric, Maayan Krispil-Alon, Vladimir Jovasevic, Kendra Parker, Thomas E. Bassett, Anna Carboncino, Anita L. Guedea, Pengfei Yi, Gal Richter-Levin, J. Tiago Gonçalves and Jelena Radulovic, 17 July 2026,
PLOS Biology
DOI: 10.1371/journal.pbio.3003908
The paper was supported by NIH grants R01MH108837 and R01MH078064 and the United States-Israel Binational Science Foundation Grant 2019261.

title: Scientists Let Nature Take Over a Former Crop Field. The Results Were Remarkable
content:
UCL researchers found that abandoned farmland can be transformed into grassland rich in wildflowers without expensive, labor-intensive seeding.
A former crop field in Norfolk was left without seed mixes, soil removal, or deep plowing. Once a year, its vegetation was cut for hay and removed. Over the next decade, wildflowers gradually returned, including
that had become rare in the surrounding landscape.
Researchers followed this transformation from 2011 through 2022 on a two-hectare field that had been abandoned in 2005. Their findings, published in
Restoration Ecology
, suggest that former farmland can develop rich plant diversity within 10 to 15 years through natural recovery supported only by traditional annual hay cutting.
That result challenges a common strategy in government-funded conservation programs, which often rely on commercially produced seed mixtures to establish new grasslands.
The question matters because wildflower meadows provide habitat for pollinating insects and other wildlife while supporting many uncommon plant species. More than 90% of these habitats have disappeared from the UK since the Second World War.
At the same time, governments across the UK and Europe are considering how to restore large areas of abandoned agricultural land. Allowing vegetation to recover naturally could offer a practical way to create species-rich grassland across broad areas without the cost and labor required by more intensive methods.
Lead author Professor Carl Sayer (UCL Geography) said: “Our study shows that resisting seeding and allowing nature to lead may be worth trying a lot more in wildflower meadow restoration. Natural plant recovery better safeguards genetic diversity than seeding and ensures that local species thrive, making meadows less generic. As things stand, the UK needs nature recovery fast, at big scales. In the push to achieve this goal, our study poses the question: should we be employing patience over seed packets more often?”
Restoring grasslands can support pollinators, birds, and mammals while protecting threatened species. These habitats may also reduce pollution and make ecosystems better able to withstand the effects of climate change.
Many restoration projects attempt to accelerate the process by sowing commercial wildflower mixtures. Some also remove topsoil or use deep plowing, approaches that require additional labor and money.
Yet researchers have conducted relatively few long-term UK studies of what happens when former farmland is managed only by cutting and removing hay. The gap exists partly because natural recovery takes years to document and requires consistent monitoring throughout that period.
The researchers studied Sayer’s Meadow in Bodham, North Norfolk. The property belongs to the father of the lead researcher and fellow coauthor Derek Sayer, allowing the annual hay cutting to continue consistently and giving the researchers access to a detailed record of how the land had previously been used.
Every two to three years, Professor Sayer and his friend Pete Robinson surveyed the field to document which plants had appeared. They walked systematically across the meadow and recorded every species they encountered.
To measure changes more precisely, they also closely examined two dozen plots, each measuring 1 square meter, positioned along two transects. Returning to the same sampling areas allowed them to track whether plant diversity was increasing rather than relying only on general impressions of the meadow.
The surveys showed a consistent rise in the number of plant species between 2011 and 2022. After approximately 10 years, the meadow contained several locally rare plants, including southern marsh orchid, greater tussock sedge, yellow rattle, and common centaury.
Within the closely monitored plots, the average number of species nearly doubled. A typical plot contained about 10 species in 2011 and almost 20 by 2022.
The researchers also recorded the exact
position of every orchid and yellow rattle plant. These species served as indicators of improving meadow conditions, but the researchers eventually stopped mapping individual plants because their numbers had grown too large to count.
How some uncommon plants reached the field remains uncertain, especially because the nearest known populations were some distance away. The researchers suggested that deer may have carried seeds into the meadow.
Professor Sayer said: “The field belongs to my family, and after an oilseed rape crop in 2005 we stopped farming it as the land was difficult to drain. I really wanted a wildflower meadow, and all advice was to seed it, but I resisted the temptation, as I have always been interested in nature’s ability to recover itself.
“When the first orchids started appearing in our surveys, we were thrilled, and now the meadow is unbelievably diverse, with thousands of orchids that delight locals in the village. Our study shows what can be achieved by a traditional hay cutting approach combined with nature’s brilliant spontaneity. A visit to the meadow is like stepping back through time.”
Reference: “No need to seed: restoring species-rich grassland on former arable fields by natural regeneration” by Carl D. Sayer, Peter Robinson, Derek C. G. Sayer and Jan C. Axmacher, 29 June 2026,
Restoration Ecology
DOI: 10.1111/rec.70487
